reasoning:
The baseline value on or around 2026-03-01 is expected to be ~4.58, which is at the lower end of its recent trading range (4.57 - 4.60 in late February). On February 27, a hot wholesale inflation report triggered a flight to safety, sending the 10-year Treasury yield below 4.00%. However, corporate spreads widened simultaneously to absorb this drop, causing the Single-A corporate yield to barely move (dipping to 4.57 and immediately rebounding to 4.58 on the 28th). 

Because inflation pressures (recently cited by Fed Chair Powell as partly due to tariffs) generally force yields higher, and because credit spreads are currently cracking and widening, there is an asymmetric risk that corporate yields will drift upward. If the equity sell-off continues, spreads will widen further; if the flight-to-safety reverses, Treasury yields will quickly rebound. This makes a short-term upward drift by March 8 slightly more likely than not (57%). Over the longer horizon by March 31, there is much greater uncertainty surrounding Fed policy and overall economic trajectory, pulling the probability closer to the coin-flip base rate (53%).
